18: etheoroun ton Satanan hos astrapen ek tou ouranou pesonta — I was watching Satan fall like lightning from heaven — the aorist pesonta (having fallen) suggests a definitive past event rather than a process; the 70's mission success is the visible sign of an already-accomplished heavenly event. Astrapen (lightning) evokes the sudden, complete, and irresistible nature of the fall. The statement is Jesus's interpretation of the exorcism-mission: what the disciples experience as individual deliverances is the symptom of a decisive cosmic defeat of the enemy already accomplished through Jesus's authority.
21: exomologoumai soi pater kyrie tou ouranou kai tes ges, hoti apekrupsas tauta apo sophon kai synetōn kai apekalypsas auta nepiois — I thank you Father Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little ones — the prayer is Luke's unique Trinitarian pericope: Jesus addresses the Father, the Spirit's enabling is implied by the eschatological joy (egalliasthato en to pneumati to hagio, v.21), and Jesus speaks as the Son who alone knows the Father (v.22). Nepiois (little children, infants, the simple) are the recipients of revelation because divine grace rather than human capacity is its source.
1: meta de tauta anedeixen ho kyrios heterous hebdomekontadyo — after this the Lord appointed seventy-two others. The textual variant between hebdomekonta (70, Alexandrian tradition) and hebdomekontadyo (72, Western tradition) is significant: 70 echoes the 70 elders of Num 11:16-17 and the Table of Nations in Gen 10 (70 in Hebrew, 72 in LXX); 72 = 6 x 12 (extending the Twelve’s mission). Both readings have ancient support. The larger mission (this group unique to Luke) extends the Twelve’s commission into the universal scope: every town and place where Jesus was about to go.
2: ho men therismos polys, hoi de ergatai oligoi — the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few. The harvest metaphor is eschatological: in the OT, harvest-day is a metaphor for the judgment/ingathering of nations (Isa 27:12; Joel 3:13; Jer 51:33). Deēthēte oun tou kyriou tou therismou (ask the Lord of the harvest) — the Lord of the harvest is YHWH (Amos 9:13); the mission workers are sent by God, not recruited by human organization. The command to pray for more workers precedes the sending, establishing prayer as the engine of mission.
3: hypagete; idou apostellō hymas hōs arnas en mesō lykōn — go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves. Apostellō (I am sending) is the commissioning verb that gives the apostoloi their name; the authority behind the sending is Jesus himself. Arnas en mesō lykōn (lambs among wolves) — the vulnerability is real and intentional; the mission is not accomplished through strength or cunning but through the willingness to be lamb-like. Isaiah 11:6 (the wolf and the lamb lying together) provides the eschatological background: the wolf-and-lamb relationship will be reversed in the kingdom, but not yet.
4: mē bastasete ballantion, mē pēran, mē hypodēmata — do not take a purse, bag, or sandals. The mission equipment list is deliberately reduced to nothing: no money-pouch (ballantion), no traveler’s bag (pēra), no extra sandals (hypodēmata). The restriction signals complete dependence on the hospitality of those who receive the message. Mēdena kata tēn hodon aspasēsthe (greet no one on the road) — the urgency of the mission precludes the extended social rituals of ancient greeting; the peace-greeting is reserved for the households that will receive it.
5: eis hēn d an eiselthēte oikian, prōton legete: eirēnē tō oikō toutō — when you enter a house, first say: Peace to this house. Eirēnē (peace, shalom) as the mission greeting is not a casual salutation but a performative pronouncement: the peace of the kingdom is being spoken over the household. The greeting is the mission in miniature: what the kingdom brings is shalom — the comprehensive wellbeing of restored covenant relationship. Whether the greeting remains or returns depends on what follows in the household’s response.
6: kai ean ekei ē huios eirēnēs — if a son of peace is there. Huios eirēnēs (son of peace) is a Semitic idiom for a person whose character is defined by peace — one prepared to receive the peace-word. The idiom is unique to Luke’s version of the mission discourse and may reflect Aramaic source material. Epanapaēsetai ep auton hē eirēnē hymōn (your peace will rest upon him) — the peace is personified as a presence that settles or departs; it has a locative quality, finding its place in a receptive person.
7: en autē de tē oikia menete, esthiontes kai pinontes ta par autōn — stay there, eating and drinking whatever they give you. The instructions against moving house-to-house prevent both the exploitation of multiple hospitalities and the social awkwardness of being a demanding guest. Axios gar ho ergatēs tou misthou autou estin (the worker deserves his wages) — the principle cited here (also 1 Tim 5:18; 1 Cor 9:14) grounds the support of mission workers in a principle of just compensation: the harvest-work has a legitimate wage. The saying may derive from an existing proverb that Jesus quotes as principle.
8: kai eis hēn an polin eiserchēsthe kai dechontai hymas, esthiete ta paratithemena hymin — when you enter a town and are welcomed, eat what is set before you. The prohibition on dietary selectivity (ta paratithemena, whatever is put before you) has early-church implications: eating whatever is served without inquiry into its ritual status (cf. 1 Cor 10:27) anticipates the Gentile mission, where Mosaic food laws would create barriers to table fellowship. Luke’s version of the mission instruction may be shaped by post-resurrection missionary experience.
9: kai therapeuete tous en autē astheneis kai legete autois: ēngiken eph hymas hē basileia tou theou — heal the sick there and tell them: The kingdom of God has come near to you. The mission action and the mission proclamation are a unity: healing is the visible sign of the kingdom’s nearness, and the verbal announcement interprets the sign. Ēngiken eph hymas (has come near upon/to you — the eph intensifying the arrival) — the kingdom’s nearness is not a vague temporal proximity but an arrival that touches the specific persons being addressed. The second-person hymas makes the announcement personal and local.
10: eis hēn d an polin eiselthēte kai mē dechontai hymas — but when you enter a town and are not welcomed. The asymmetry between welcome and rejection is a mission reality; the instructions for rejection are as important as those for welcome. The dust-wiping action (ton koniorton ton kollēthenta hēmin ek tēs poleōs hymōn apomassometha hymin) that follows is a prophetic symbolic act: Jews shook dust from their sandals when leaving Gentile territory to keep the holy land clean; the mission workers perform the reverse, treating the rejecting Jewish town as if it were pagan territory.
11: plēn touto ginōskete hoti ēngiken hē basileia tou theou — yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near. The kingdom’s nearness is proclaimed even to those who reject it: the announcement is not contingent on reception. The combination of the dust-wiping sign of judgment and the proclamation of the kingdom’s nearness is deliberately paradoxical: the kingdom comes near even to those who refuse it, which is precisely what makes their rejection so serious. The nearness increases both the opportunity and the culpability.
12: legō hymin hoti Sodumois en tē hēmera ekeinē anektoteron estai ē tē polei ekeinē — it will be more bearable for Sodom on that day than for that town. Hē hēmera ekeinē (that day) is the eschatological day of judgment (Amos 5:18; Joel 1:15; Isa 2:12). Sodom’s sin was hospitality-failure (Gen 19) — the rejecting towns replicate Sodom’s sin in refusing the messengers who bring the kingdom. The comparison (Sodom vs. the towns that rejected Jesus’s mission) is the most severe judgment-saying in the tradition: greater light means greater accountability.
13: ouai soi, Chorazin! ouai soi, Bēthsaïda! — woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! The woe-oracle (ouai) against Galilean towns is unique in its retrospective naming of miracles not recorded in the Gospels (hoti ei en Tyrō kai Sidōni egenēthēsan hai dynameis hai genomenai en hymin). Dynameis (miracles, acts of power) performed in Chorazin and Bethsaida are not narrated in Luke; Jesus’s statement implies a much larger body of activity than the Gospel records. Tyre and Sidon, historically condemned by the prophets (Ezek 28-29; Isa 23), are now presented as more responsive than the privileged Galilean towns.
14: Tyrō kai Sidōni anektoteron estai en tē krisei ē hymin — it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment than for you. The comparative judgment principle (anektoteron, more bearable) assumes graduated accountability: judgment is proportional to the revelation received. The Gentile cities of the Phoenician coast, without Israel’s covenant history, will fare better than Israelite towns that witnessed the kingdom’s signs and rejected them. The principle challenges ethnic covenant presumption: proximity to Jesus increases responsibility.
15: kai sy, Kapharnaoum, mē heōs ouranou hypsōthēsē; heōs tou hadou katabibasthēsē — and you, Capernaum, will you be lifted to the heavens? No, you will go down to Hades. The language is a deliberate echo of Isaiah 14:13-15 (the taunt against the king of Babylon: “You said in your heart, ‘I will ascend to the heavens’... but you are brought down to the realm of the dead”). Capernaum is Jesus’s base of operations (4:31) — the most privileged town in Galilee in terms of exposure to his ministry. The Babel-reversal pattern (exaltation presumed, descent decreed) is the judgment on privilege misused.
16: ho akouōn hymōn emou akouei kai ho atheton hymas eme athetei, ho de eme atheton athetei ton aposteilanta me — whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; but whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me. The chain of representation (disciple → Jesus → the Father) is the apostolic authority principle: the apostolos (sent one) represents the sender’s person and authority. Athetei (rejects, sets aside as invalid — the verb used for nullifying a legal agreement) — to reject the mission workers is not a personal slight but a rejection of the divine sending-authority behind them.
17: hypestrepsan de hoi hebdomekontadyo meta charas legontes: kyrie, kai ta daimonia hypotassetai hēmin en tō onomati sou — the seventy-two returned with joy saying: Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name! The exorcistic authority of the mission workers (hypotassetai, present tense — is being subjected) is exercised en tō onomati sou (in your name) — the name as the medium of delegated authority. Jesus’s response will redirect the joy from power-over-demons to the greater gift: names written in heaven (v.20).
19: idou dedōka hymin tēn exousian tou patein epanō opheōn kai skorpiōn kai epi pasan tēn dynamin tou echthrou — I have given you authority to tread on snakes and scorpions and over all the power of the enemy. Exousia (authority, right to act) is distinguished from dynamis (power); the disciples have been given the authority that underlies the power. The serpent-and-scorpion imagery invokes Psalm 91:13 (trampling the lion and the serpent) and the Genesis 3:15 enmity between the seed and the serpent. Ouden hymas ou mē adikēsē (nothing will harm you) — the promise is mission-specific, not a general immunity guarantee.
20: plēn en toutō mē chairete hoti ta pneumata hymin hypotassetai — however, do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you. The redirected joy is a priority statement: exorcistic success is real but penultimate. Chairete de hoti ta onomata hymōn eggegraptai en tois ouranois (rejoice that your names are written in heaven) — the heavenly enrollment recalls the Book of Life (Exod 32:32; Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1; Rev 3:5). The ultimate ground of joy is not what the disciples accomplish but what God has already done: inscribing their names in the divine ledger. Power over demons is a gift; heavenly registration is an identity.
22: panta moi paredothē hypo tou patros mou — all things have been committed to me by my Father. The Johannine-sounding logion (also Matt 11:27) in the Synoptic tradition: paredothē (has been handed over, entrusted — the same root as paradidōmi used for betrayal and for the Lord’s Supper tradition) emphasizes the totality and the relational dynamic of the transfer. Oudeis ginōskei tis estin ho huios ei mē ho patēr kai tis estin ho patēr ei mē ho huios — the mutual exclusivity of Sonship-knowing (only the Father knows the Son fully; only the Son knows the Father fully) is the ground of revelatory dependence: encountering the Father requires going through the Son.
23: makarioi hoi ophthalmoi hoi blepontes ha blepete — blessed are the eyes that see what you see. The beatitude on the disciples’ sight (privately, kat idian, said to the Twelve) is the positive counterpart to v.15’s woe on Capernaum. Ophthalmoi blepontes (eyes that see) — the eyes of the disciples who see the kingdom arriving are blessed with a privilege that the greatest OT figures could only anticipate. The sight is not merely physical presence but kingdom-recognition: they see the signs and understand what they signify.
24: polloi prophētai kai basileis ēthelēsan idein ha hymeis blepete kai ouk eidan — many prophets and kings wanted to see what you see but did not see it. The prophets and kings of Israel desired the messianic era with anticipatory longing: Abraham desired to see Jesus’s day and saw it and was glad (John 8:56); Daniel longed for the interpretation of his visions (Dan 12:8). The disciples’ blessing is not their own achievement but their historical position: they are the generation at the hinge of the ages when what was promised has arrived.
25: kai idou nomikos tis anestē ekpeirazōn auton legōn: didaskale, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō — an expert in the law stood up to test him: Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Ekpeirazōn (testing, putting to the test — with hostile intent, cf. the Deuteronomy 6:16 prohibition on testing God) frames the encounter as an adversarial one. Zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō (inherit eternal life) — the question is correctly framed as inheritance (received from a superior, the Father) rather than achievement (earned by works). Jesus turns the question back on the questioner: what does the Law itself say?
26: ho de eipen pros auton: en tō nomō ti gegraptai; pōs anaginōskeis — he replied: What is written in the Law? How do you read it? Jesus’s counter-question is a rabbinical technique: direct the student to the source and ask for their interpretation. Pōs anaginōskeis (how do you read it? — literally, how do you recognize/read it) — the emphasis is on the hermeneutical method: the letter of the text and its interpretive application. Jesus grants that the answer to the life-question lies within the Torah itself; his role is to question the mode of application.
27: ho de apokritheis eipen: agapēseis kyrion ton theon sou ex holēs tēs kardias sou — he answered: Love the Lord your God with all your heart. The combination of Deuteronomy 6:5 (the Shema love-command) and Leviticus 19:18 (neighbor-love) as the Torah’s double summary is known from rabbinic tradition; Jesus affirms the lawyer’s answer as correct. The four faculties (kardia, psychē, ischus, dianoia — heart, soul, strength, mind) are Luke’s expansion of the Deuteronomy text’s three (heart, soul, strength), emphasizing total engagement of all human capacities in the love of God.
28: orthos apekrithēs; touto poiei kai zēsē — you have answered correctly; do this and you will live. Jesus’s validation of the double love-command is without qualification. Touto poiei (do this — present imperative, continuous action) and zēsē (you will live) — the connection between doing and living is the Leviticus 18:5 formula (“keep my decrees and ordinances; the person who obeys them will live”). The simplicity of the answer is precisely what the lawyer finds uncomfortable: the question “who is my neighbor?” follows because the answer is too simple, too demanding.
29: ho de thelōn dikaiōsai heauton eipen pros ton Iēsoun: kai tis estin mou ho plesion — but he wanted to justify himself, so he asked Jesus: And who is my neighbour? Thelōn dikaiōsai heauton (wanting to justify himself) — the self-justification motive is Luke’s psychological insight into the question’s origin. The question “who is my neighbor?” is a definitional question seeking to limit the obligation: if neighbor can be defined narrowly (fellow Jew, covenant-community member), the love-command has manageable scope. Jesus’s parable will flip the question from “who is my neighbor?” to “who acted as neighbor?” — from definition to practice.
30: hypolabōn ho Iēsous eipen: anthrōpos tis katebainen apo Ierousalēm eis Ierichō — Jesus replied: A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho. The Jerusalem-to-Jericho road descends roughly 3,300 feet in 17 miles through rocky, robber-prone terrain — the detail is geographically precise. The man is unidentified (anthrōpos tis, a certain man) — no ethnic marker, no religious status; the story’s universalizing strategy begins with the nameless victim. Lēstais periepesan (fell into the hands of robbers) — the aorist signals the sudden, violent nature of the attack; the robbers strip, beat, and leave him hēmithanē (half-dead).
31: kata synkyrion de hiereus tis katebainen en tē hodō ekeinē, kai idōn auton antiparēlthen — a priest happened to be going down the same road, and when he saw the man, he passed by on the other side. Kata synkyrion (by coincidence, by chance — a hapax in the NT) indicates an unplanned encounter; the priest did not seek this situation. The priest’s reason for passing is not stated; interpreters have suggested concern about ritual impurity from contact with a potentially dead body (Lev 21:1-3). But Jesus does not supply the reason — the parable focuses on the action: seeing and passing by, rather than seeing and responding.
32: homoiōs de kai Leuitēs kata ton topon elthōn kai idōn antiparēlthen — so too, a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side. The Levite’s response mirrors the priest’s; the repetition reinforces the expected pattern. The audience would anticipate a third Israelite — the priest-Levite-Israelite triplet is a known Jewish storytelling pattern. The shock of the parable’s structure is the substitution of a Samaritan for the expected Israelite in the third position: the religiously and ethnically despised figure becomes the model of neighbor-love.
33: Samaritēs de tis hodeuōn ēlthen kat auton kai idōn esplanchnisthē — but a Samaritan, as he traveled, came where the man was; and when he saw him, he had compassion. Samaritēs — the Samaritan is the expected villain of the story from a Jewish audience’s perspective (John 4:9; 8:48). Esplanchnisthē (had compassion, was moved in the bowels — Luke’s characteristic compassion verb; see 7:13; 15:20) — the Samaritan’s response is visceral, immediate, and identical to the language used for Jesus himself and the father in the prodigal. The most despised figure in the story has the most God-like response.
34: kai proselthōn katedēsen ta traumata autou epicheōn elaion kai oinon — he went to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. The medical treatment is detailed: oil (for soothing and antiseptic properties) and wine (for cleansing, as an astringent) are the first-century field medicine. The Samaritan uses his own traveling supplies for the stranger. Epibibasas de auton epi to idion ktēnos ēgagen auton eis pandocheion kai epemeleuthē autou — placing him on his own animal, taking him to an inn, and caring for him: three further acts of practical self-expenditure for the other. Each action costs him something.
35: kai epi tēn aurion ekbalōn duo dēnaria edōken tō pandochei kai eipen: epimeleuthēti autou — the next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper. Two denarii represented two days’ wages — sufficient for several weeks of basic board at the time. Ho ti an prosdapanēsēs egō en tō epanercesthai mou apodōsō soi (whatever extra expense you incur, I will repay you when I return) — the open-ended financial commitment goes beyond the immediate payment; the Samaritan takes personal financial responsibility for the stranger’s ongoing care. The parable ends not with the resolution of the victim’s condition but with the open commitment of the helper.
36: tis toutōn tōn triōn plēsion dokei soi gegenēsthai tou empesontos eis tous lēstas — which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers? The question shifts the frame entirely: the lawyer asked “who is my neighbor?” (a question about the object of obligation); Jesus asks “who became neighbor?” (a question about the subject of action). The neighbor is not defined by proximity, ethnicity, or religion but by the practice of mercy. The question forces the lawyer to answer from the logic of the story rather than from his own prejudice.
37: ho de eipen: ho poiēsas to eleos met autou; eipen de autō ho Iēsous: poreuou kai sy poiei homoiōs — the expert replied: The one who had mercy on him. Jesus told him: Go and do likewise. The lawyer cannot bring himself to say “the Samaritan” — he uses the functional description ho poiēsas to eleos (the one who showed mercy). Eleos (mercy, steadfast love — the LXX rendering of hesed) is the covenant-quality that the Samaritan exemplifies. Jesus’s conclusion poreuou kai sy poiei homoiōs (go and you do likewise) applies the parable directly: the answer to “what must I do?” is “be the Samaritan.”
38: en de tō poreuesthai autous autos eisēlthen eis kōmēn tina; gynē de tis onomati Martha hypedexato auton — as they were on their way, Jesus came to a village where a woman named Martha welcomed him. The Martha-Mary episode follows the Good Samaritan without transition, creating a diptych: active service (the Samaritan) and contemplative reception (Mary). Hypedexato (welcomed, received as a guest — a formal hospitality term) — Martha is the householder who initiates the welcome; her name (Martha, Aramaic for “mistress/lady of the house”) identifies her social role.
39: kai tēde ēn adelphē kaloumenē Mariam, hē kai parakathisasa pros tous podas tou kyriou ēkouen ton logon autou — she had a sister called Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet listening to what he said. Parakathisasa pros tous podas (sat alongside at the feet) — the posture of a student receiving instruction from a teacher; “at the feet” is the technical term for the disciple’s position (Acts 22:3 — Paul “educated at the feet of Gamaliel”). Mary’s posture is that of a disciple, not a servant; she has taken the role of a learner, which in the cultural context was typically reserved for men. Ton logon autou (his word) — the singular logos as the content of the teaching session.
40: hē de Martha periespa peri pollēn diakonion — Martha was distracted by all the preparations. Periespa (was distracted, pulled away in different directions — a compound intensifying the pulling-apart image) conveys the scattered, anxious state of someone managing many details at once. Diakonios (service, preparation — the same root as diakonos, deacon) is the domestic service Martha is providing, which is itself a genuine act of hospitality. Her complaint to Jesus (kyrion, ou melei soi — don’t you care, Lord?) reveals the pressure she is under and the resentment accumulating toward her sister.
41: apokritheis de eipen autē ho kyrios: Martha Martha, merimnās kai thorybazē peri polla — the Lord answered: Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things. The double address (Martha Martha) is tender and searching — the same pattern as Jesus’s address to Saul (Saul Saul, Acts 9:4) and to Jerusalem (Ierousalēm Ierousalēm, 13:34). Merimnās (you are anxious/worried — the worry-word that appears in the cares-and-riches parable, 8:14; and in 12:22,25,26 about not worrying about life’s necessities) and thorybazē (you are troubled, disturbed) — the two verbs describe the internal state of Martha’s distraction.
42: henos de estin chreia; Mariam gar tēn agathēn merida exelexato — but few things are needed — or indeed only one. Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken away from her. The textual variants here are significant: some manuscripts read “few things are needed” (oligōn de estin chreia), others “only one thing is needed” (henos), and some combine both. The “one thing” reading (preferred) identifies the single necessary thing as the hearing of Jesus’s word — the logos that Mary is receiving. Hē agathē meris (the good portion/share) is wisdom language (Ps 16:5-6); Mary has chosen the better portion — the one that will not be taken away even when the meal-service ends.
